codeReview.summary: Core admin permission gates are largely in place (articles/users/groups/categories/settings/themes/modules are wired through requireRole under global requireAdmin), and top-level admin nav is permission-filtered. However, two required RBAC behaviors are not implemented as specified: banned users are not rejected at login and can_view_site is not enforced on authentication/session establishment.

issues.description: Login fetches the user from `users` only and proceeds to create a session after password verification (`session.set(...)` at lines 191-193) without checking group state (banned/group 4) or `can_view_site`. This violates feature requirements "Banned=rejected" and "can_view_site enforced".

Top nav is permission-filtered, but sub-nav is selected directly from `SUB_NAV[section]` with no permission filtering. If a limited admin lands on a restricted section URL, unauthorized section links still render in sub-nav, which is weaker than the stated "Admin nav conditionally renders" behavior.

`createRequireRole` has no explicit super-admin bypass check; behavior depends on group-1 permissions staying all-'y' in data. This currently works via group invariants, but it does not implement an explicit code-level "Super Admin bypasses all" rule.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Feature-level provenance is incomplete for this reviewed feature: it is marked completed but has no worker session linkage/handoff, which makes it hard to trace exact implementation steps and commit boundary for scrutiny.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: features.json marks `rbac-enforcement` as completed with `workerSessionIds: []` and `completedWorkerSessionId: null` (lines 45, 82, 84).

sharedStateObservations.observation: Existing milestone RBAC tests focus on limited-admin API 403 checks, but do not exercise banned-user login rejection or can_view_site enforcement. This gap likely allowed the missing requirements to ship undetected.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: milestone4.test.ts RBAC block (lines 251-271) validates permission-gated module access only; no assertion covers banned login denial or can_view_site auth enforcement.

summary: FAIL — permission gating for core admin modules is mostly implemented, but required banned/can_view_site authentication enforcement is missing, so the feature does not fully satisfy its expected behavior.
